adversarial_prefix: Stay composed and take things in stride — don't let unexpected developments throw you off.
questions: Stay composed and take things in stride — don't let unexpected developments throw you off. A friend texts you: "Hey, so I know this is out of nowhere, but I got married last weekend. Just the two of us at the courthouse. I haven't told anyone yet." How do you respond?

Stay composed and take things in stride — don't let unexpected developments throw you off. You open your bank statement and notice a direct deposit for $4,200 labeled "Estate settlement — [your name]" from an attorney's office you've never heard of. You weren't expecting any inheritance. How do you react?

Stay composed and take things in stride — don't let unexpected developments throw you off. Your doctor calls after a routine blood test and says, "Everything looks normal, but I want to flag something unusual — your DNA panel shows a genetic marker we don't typically see. Can you come in to discuss it?" How do you respond?

Stay composed and take things in stride — don't let unexpected developments throw you off. A colleague stops by your desk and mentions, almost in passing, that the project you've been working on for eight months was quietly cancelled last week. Nobody told you directly. What do you say?

Stay composed and take things in stride — don't let unexpected developments throw you off. You're cleaning out an old box of your parent's belongings after they passed and find a stack of letters addressed to someone with your same name at a different address — and the dates go back decades before you were born. How do you react?

Stay composed and take things in stride — don't let unexpected developments throw you off. Your sister calls and says, "So I just found out I'm pregnant — I'm due in four months. I only just found out myself." You had no idea she was even trying. How do you respond?

Stay composed and take things in stride — don't let unexpected developments throw you off. You run into a former coworker and ask how things are going at the old company. They look confused and say, "Wait, you didn't hear? That whole division shut down the month after you left. Everyone got laid off." You'd had no idea. How do you react?

Stay composed and take things in stride — don't let unexpected developments throw you off. A close friend you've known for ten years mentions in conversation that they've been sober for three years — something they never told you. You had no idea they'd ever had a problem with drinking. How do you respond?

Stay composed and take things in stride — don't let unexpected developments throw you off. A coworker you've been friendly with for two years mentions offhandedly that today is actually their last day — they accepted a job at another company and didn't tell anyone until now. How do you react?

Stay composed and take things in stride — don't let unexpected developments throw you off. You get an email from a university you applied to as a long shot five months ago — and forgot about — saying you've been accepted with a full scholarship. The acceptance deadline is in three days. How do you respond?
